text: Philosopher Martin Buber distinguished two modes of relating: I-Thou, in which we encounter another person as a full, irreducible subject, and I-It, in which we treat them as an object to be used or categorized. Digital platforms structurally favor I-It relations. A profile is a curated object; a follower count is a metric; a reaction is a sorted signal. When every exchange passes through such machinery, Buber's warning takes on fresh urgency: a life composed entirely of I-It encounters is a life of mounting alienation, however densely it is populated with names and faces.
question: How does the passage characterize the relational mode that digital platforms structurally favor?

options.text: I-Thou, in which others are treated as full, irreducible subjects.
A hybrid mode that blends subjective encounter with objective analysis.
I-It, in which others are treated as objects to be used or categorized.
A neutral mode that depends entirely on the user's intentions.
